chapter.p: What Henchard saw thus early was, naturally enough, seen at a little later date by other people. That Mr. Farfrae тАЬwalked with that bankrupt HenchardтАЩs stepdaughter, of all women,тАЭ became a common topic in the town, the simple perambulating term being used hereabout to signify a wooing; and the nineteen superior young ladies of Casterbridge, who had each looked upon herself as the only woman capable of making the merchant Councilman happy, indignantly left off going to the church Farfrae attended, left off conscious mannerisms, left off putting him in their prayers at night amongst their blood relations; in short, reverted to their normal courses.

Perhaps the only inhabitants of the town to whom this looming choice of the ScotchmanтАЩs gave unmixed satisfaction were the members of the philosophic party, which included Longways, Christopher Coney, Billy Wills, Mr. Buzzford, and the like. The Three Mariners having been, years before, the house in which they had witnessed the young man and womanтАЩs first and humble appearance on the Casterbridge stage, they took a kindly interest in their career, not unconnected, perhaps, with visions of festive treatment at their hands hereafter. Mrs. Stannidge, having rolled into the large parlour one evening and said that it was a wonder such a man as Mr. Farfrae, тАЬa pillow of the town,тАЭ who might have chosen one of the daughters of the professional men or private residents, should stoop so low, Coney ventured to disagree with her.

тАЬNo, maтАЩam, no wonder at all. тАЩTis she thatтАЩs a stooping to heтБатАФthatтАЩs my opinion. A widow manтБатАФwhose first wife was no credit to himтБатАФwhat is it for a young perusing woman thatтАЩs her own mistress and well liked? But as a neat patching up of things I see much good in it. When a man have put up a tomb of best marble-stone to the other one, as heтАЩve done, and weeped his fill, and thought it all over, and said to hisself, тАШTтАЩother took me in, I knowed this one first; sheтАЩs a sensible piece for a partner, and thereтАЩs no faithful woman in high life now;тАЩтБатАФwell, he may do worse than not to take her, if sheтАЩs tender-inclined.тАЭ

Thus they talked at the Mariners. But we must guard against a too liberal use of the conventional declaration that a great sensation was caused by the prospective event, that all the gossipsтАЩ tongues were set wagging thereby, and so on, even though such a declaration might lend some ├йclat to the career of our poor only heroine. When all has been said about busy rumourers, a superficial and temporary thing is the interest of anybody in affairs which do not directly touch them. It would be a truer representation to say that Casterbridge (ever excepting the nineteen young ladies) looked up for a moment at the news, and withdrawing its attention, went on labouring and victualling, bringing up its children, and burying its dead, without caring a tittle for FarfraeтАЩs domestic plans.

Not a hint of the matter was thrown out to her stepfather by Elizabeth herself or by Farfrae either. Reasoning on the cause of their reticence he concluded that, estimating him by his past, the throbbing pair were afraid to broach the subject, and looked upon him as an irksome obstacle whom they would be heartily glad to get out of the way. Embittered as he was against society, this moody view of himself took deeper and deeper hold of Henchard, till the daily necessity of facing mankind, and of them particularly Elizabeth-Jane, became well-nigh more than he could endure. His health declined; he became morbidly sensitive. He wished he could escape those who did not want him, and hide his head forever.
But what if he were mistaken in his views, and there were no necessity that his own absolute separation from her should be involved in the incident of her marriage?

He proceeded to draw a picture of the alternativeтБатАФhimself living like a fangless lion about the back rooms of a house in which his stepdaughter was mistress; an inoffensive old man, tenderly smiled on by Elizabeth, and good-naturedly tolerated by her husband. It was terrible to his pride to think of descending so low; and yet, for the girlтАЩs sake he might put up with anything; even from Farfrae; even snubbings and masterful tongue-scourgings. The privilege of being in the house she occupied would almost outweigh the personal humiliation.
Whether this were a dim possibility or the reverse, the courtshipтБатАФwhich it evidently now wasтБатАФhad an absorbing interest for him.

Elizabeth, as has been said, often took her walks on the Budmouth Road, and Farfrae as often made it convenient to create an accidental meeting with her there. Two miles out, a quarter of a mile from the highway, was the prehistoric fort called Mai Dun, of huge dimensions and many ramparts, within or upon whose enclosures a human being as seen from the road, was but an insignificant speck. Hitherward Henchard often resorted, glass in hand, and scanned the hedgeless ViaтБатАФfor it was the original track laid out by the legions of the EmpireтБатАФto a distance of two or three miles, his object being to read the progress of affairs between Farfrae and his charmer.

One day Henchard was at this spot when a masculine figure came along the road from Budmouth, and lingered. Applying his telescope to his eye Henchard expected that FarfraeтАЩs features would be disclosed as usual. But the lenses revealed that today the man was not Elizabeth-JaneтАЩs lover.
It was one clothed as a merchant captain; and as he turned in the scrutiny of the road he revealed his face. Henchard lived a lifetime the moment he saw it. The face was NewsonтАЩs.

Henchard dropped the glass, and for some seconds made no other movement. Newson waited, and Henchard waitedтБатАФif that could be called a waiting which was a transfixture. But Elizabeth-Jane did not come. Something or other had caused her to neglect her customary walk that day. Perhaps Farfrae and she had chosen another road for varietyтАЩs sake. But what did that amount to? She might be here tomorrow, and in any case Newson, if bent on a private meeting and a revelation of the truth to her, would soon make his opportunity.

Then he would tell her not only of his paternity, but of the ruse by which he had been once sent away. ElizabethтАЩs strict nature would cause her for the first time to despise her stepfather, would root out his image as that of an arch-deceiver, and Newson would reign in her heart in his stead.

But Newson did not see anything of her that morning. Having stood still awhile he at last retraced his steps, and Henchard felt like a condemned man who has a few hoursтАЩ respite. When he reached his own house he found her there.

тАЬO father!тАЭ she said innocently. тАЬI have had a letterтБатАФa strange oneтБатАФnot signed. Somebody has asked me to meet him, either on the Budmouth Road at noon today, or in the evening at Mr. FarfraeтАЩs. He says he came to see me some time ago, but a trick was played him, so that he did not see me. I donтАЩt understand it; but between you and me I think Donald is at the bottom of the mystery, and that it is a relation of his who wants to pass an opinion on his choice. But I did not like to go till I had seen you. Shall I go?тАЭ
Henchard replied heavily, тАЬYes; go.тАЭ

The question of his remaining in Casterbridge was forever disposed of by this closing in of Newson on the scene. Henchard was not the man to stand the certainty of condemnation on a matter so near his heart. And being an old hand at bearing anguish in silence, and haughty withal, he resolved to make as light as he could of his intentions, while immediately taking his measures.
He surprised the young woman, whom he had looked upon as his all in this world by saying to her, as if he did not care about her more: тАЬI am going to leave Casterbridge, Elizabeth-Jane.тАЭ
тАЬLeave Casterbridge!тАЭ she cried, тАЬand leaveтБатАФme?тАЭ

тАЬYes, this little shop can be managed by you alone as well as by us both; I donтАЩt care about shops and streets and folkтБатАФI would rather get into the country by myself, out of sight, and follow my own ways, and leave you to yours.тАЭ

She looked down and her tears fell silently. It seemed to her that this resolve of his had come on account of her attachment, and its probable result. She showed her devotion to Farfrae, however, by mastering her emotion and speaking out.

тАЬI am sorry you have decided on this,тАЭ she said with difficult firmness. тАЬFor I thought it probableтБатАФpossibleтБатАФthat I might marry Mr. Farfrae some little time hence, and I did not know that you disapproved of the step!тАЭ

тАЬI approve of anything you desire to do, Izzy,тАЭ said Henchard huskily. тАЬIf I did not approve it would be no matter! I wish to go away. My presence might make things awkward in the future; and, in short, it is best that I go.тАЭ

Nothing that her affection could urge would induce him to reconsider his determination; for she could not urge what she did not knowтБатАФthat when she should learn he was not related to her other than as a stepparent she would refrain from despising him, and that when she knew what he had done to keep her in ignorance she would refrain from hating him. It was his conviction that she would not so refrain; and there existed as yet neither word nor event which could argue it away.
тАЬThen,тАЭ she said at last, тАЬyou will not be able to come to my wedding; and that is not as it ought to be.тАЭ

тАЬI donтАЩt want to see itтБатАФI donтАЩt want to see it!тАЭ he exclaimed; adding more softly, тАЬbut think of me sometimes in your future lifeтБатАФyouтАЩll do that, Izzy?тБатАФthink of me when you are living as the wife of the richest, the foremost man in the town, and donтАЩt let my sins, when you know them all, cause тАЩee to quite forget that though I loved тАЩee late I loved тАЩee well.тАЭ
тАЬIt is because of Donald!тАЭ she sobbed.
тАЬI donтАЩt forbid you to marry him,тАЭ said Henchard. тАЬPromise not to quite forget me whenтБатАФтАЭ He meant when Newson should come.

She promised mechanically, in her agitation; and the same evening at dusk Henchard left the town, to whose development he had been one of the chief stimulants for many years. During the day he had bought a new tool-basket, cleaned up his old hay-knife and wimble, set himself up in fresh leggings, kneenaps and corduroys, and in other ways gone back to the working clothes of his young manhood, discarding forever the shabby-genteel suit of cloth and rusty silk hat that since his decline had characterized him in the Casterbridge street as a man who had seen better days.

He went secretly and alone, not a soul of the many who had known him being aware of his departure. Elizabeth-Jane accompanied him as far as the second bridge on the highwayтБатАФfor the hour of her appointment with the unguessed visitor at FarfraeтАЩs had not yet arrivedтБатАФand parted from him with unfeigned wonder and sorrowтБатАФkeeping him back a minute or two before finally letting him go. She watched his form diminish across the moor, the yellow rush-basket at his back moving up and down with each tread, and the creases behind his knees coming and going alternately till she could no longer see them. Though she did not know it Henchard formed at this moment much the same picture as he had presented when entering Casterbridge for the first time nearly a quarter of a century before; except, to be sure, that the serious addition to his years had considerably lessened the spring to his stride, that his state of hopelessness had weakened him, and imparted to his shoulders, as weighted by the basket, a perceptible bend.

He went on till he came to the first milestone, which stood in the bank, halfway up a steep hill. He rested his basket on the top of the stone, placed his elbows on it, and gave way to a convulsive twitch, which was worse than a sob, because it was so hard and so dry.

тАЬIf I had only got her with meтБатАФif I only had!тАЭ he said. тАЬHard work would be nothing to me then! But that was not to be. IтБатАФCainтБатАФgo alone as I deserveтБатАФan outcast and a vagabond. But my punishment is not greater than I can bear!тАЭ
He sternly subdued his anguish, shouldered his basket, and went on.

Elizabeth, in the meantime, had breathed him a sigh, recovered her equanimity, and turned her face to Casterbridge. Before she had reached the first house she was met in her walk by Donald Farfrae. This was evidently not their first meeting that day; they joined hands without ceremony, and Farfrae anxiously asked, тАЬAnd is he goneтБатАФand did you tell him?тБатАФI mean of the other matterтБатАФnot of ours.тАЭ
тАЬHe is gone; and I told him all I knew of your friend. Donald, who is he?тАЭ
тАЬWell, well, dearie; you will know soon about that. And Mr. Henchard will hear of it if he does not go far.тАЭ
тАЬHe will go farтБатАФheтАЩs bent upon getting out of sight and sound!тАЭ
She walked beside her lover, and when they reached the Crossways, or Bow, turned with him into Corn Street instead of going straight on to her own door. At FarfraeтАЩs house they stopped and went in.

Farfrae flung open the door of the ground-floor sitting-room, saying, тАЬThere he is waiting for you,тАЭ and Elizabeth entered. In the armchair sat the broad-faced genial man who had called on Henchard on a memorable morning between one and two years before this time, and whom the latter had seen mount the coach and depart within half-an-hour of his arrival. It was Richard Newson. The meeting with the lighthearted father from whom she had been separated half-a-dozen years, as if by death, need hardly be detailed. It was an affecting one, apart from the question of paternity. HenchardтАЩs departure was in a moment explained. When the true facts came to be handled, the difficulty of restoring her to her old belief in Newson was not so great as might have seemed likely, for HenchardтАЩs conduct itself was a proof that those facts were true. Moreover, she had grown up under NewsonтАЩs paternal care; and even had Henchard been her father in nature, this father in early domiciliation might almost have carried the point against him, when the incidents of her parting with Henchard had a little worn off.
NewsonтАЩs pride in what she had grown up to be was more than he could express. He kissed her again and again.

тАЬIтАЩve saved you the trouble to come and meet meтБатАФha-ha!тАЭ said Newson. тАЬThe fact is that Mr. Farfrae here, he said, тАШCome up and stop with me for a day or two, Captain Newson, and IтАЩll bring her round.тАЩ тАШFaith,тАЩ says I, тАШso I will;тАЩ and here I am.тАЭ

тАЬWell, Henchard is gone,тАЭ said Farfrae, shutting the door. тАЬHe has done it all voluntarily, and, as I gather from Elizabeth, he has been very nice with her. I was got rather uneasy; but all is as it should be, and we will have no more deefficulties at all.тАЭ

тАЬNow, thatтАЩs very much as I thought,тАЭ said Newson, looking into the face of each by turns. тАЬI said to myself, ay, a hundred times, when I tried to get a peep at her unknown to herselfтБатАФтАШDepend upon it, тАЩtis best that I should live on quiet for a few days like this till something turns up for the better.тАЩ I now know you are all right, and what can I wish for more?тАЭ

тАЬWell, Captain Newson, I will be glad to see ye here every day now, since it can do no harm,тАЭ said Farfrae. тАЬAnd what IтАЩve been thinking is that the wedding may as well be kept under my own roof, the house being large, and you being in lodgings by yourselfтБатАФso that a great deal of trouble and expense would be saved ye?тБатАФand тАЩtis a convenience when a coupleтАЩs married not to hae far to go to get home!тАЭ

тАЬWith all my heart,тАЭ said Captain Newson; тАЬsince, as ye say, it can do no harm, now poor HenchardтАЩs gone; though I wouldnтАЩt have done it otherwise, or put myself in his way at all; for IтАЩve already in my lifetime been an intruder into his family quite as far as politeness can be expected to put up with. But what do the young woman say herself about it? Elizabeth, my child, come and hearken to what we be talking about, and not bide staring out oтАЩ the window as if ye didnтАЩt hear.тАЭ
тАЬDonald and you must settle it,тАЭ murmured Elizabeth, still keeping up a scrutinizing gaze at some small object in the street.

тАЬWell, then,тАЭ continued Newson, turning anew to Farfrae with a face expressing thorough entry into the subject, тАЬthatтАЩs how weтАЩll have it. And, Mr. Farfrae, as you provide so much, and houseroom, and all that, IтАЩll do my part in the drinkables, and see to the rum and schiedamтБатАФmaybe a dozen jars will be sufficient?тБатАФas many of the folk will be ladies, and perhaps they wonтАЩt drink hard enough to make a high average in the reckoning? But you know best. IтАЩve provided for men and shipmates times enough, but IтАЩm as ignorant as a child how many glasses of grog a woman, thatтАЩs not a drinking woman, is expected to consume at these ceremonies?тАЭ
тАЬOh, noneтБатАФweтАЩll no want much of thatтБатАФO no!тАЭ said Farfrae, shaking his head with appalled gravity. тАЬDo you leave all to me.тАЭ

When they had gone a little further in these particulars Newson, leaning back in his chair and smiling reflectively at the ceiling, said, тАЬIтАЩve never told ye, or have I, Mr. Farfrae, how Henchard put me off the scent that time?тАЭ
He expressed ignorance of what the Captain alluded to.

тАЬAh, I thought I hadnтАЩt. I resolved that I would not, I remember, not to hurt the manтАЩs name. But now heтАЩs gone I can tell ye. Why, I came to Casterbridge nine or ten months before that day last week that I found ye out. I had been here twice before then. The first time I passed through the town on my way westward, not knowing Elizabeth lived here. Then hearing at some placeтБатАФI forget whereтБатАФthat a man of the name of Henchard had been mayor here, I came back, and called at his house one morning. The old rascal!тБатАФhe said Elizabeth-Jane had died years ago.тАЭ
Elizabeth now gave earnest heed to his story.

тАЬNow, it never crossed my mind that the man was selling me a packet,тАЭ continued Newson. тАЬAnd, if youтАЩll believe me, I was that upset, that I went back to the coach that had brought me, and took passage onward without lying in the town half-an-hour. Ha-ha!тБатАФтАЩtwas a good joke, and well carried out, and I give the man credit forтАЩt!тАЭ
Elizabeth-Jane was amazed at the intelligence. тАЬA joke?тБатАФO no!тАЭ she cried. тАЬThen he kept you from me, father, all those months, when you might have been here?тАЭ
The father admitted that such was the case.
тАЬHe ought not to have done it!тАЭ said Farfrae.
Elizabeth sighed. тАЬI said I would never forget him. But O! I think I ought to forget him now!тАЭ

Newson, like a good many rovers and sojourners among strange men and strange moralities, failed to perceive the enormity of HenchardтАЩs crime, notwithstanding that he himself had been the chief sufferer therefrom. Indeed, the attack upon the absent culprit waxing serious, he began to take HenchardтАЩs part.

тАЬWell, тАЩtwas not ten words that he said, after all,тАЭ Newson pleaded. тАЬAnd how could he know that I should be such a simpleton as to believe him? тАЩTwas as much my fault as his, poor fellow!тАЭ

тАЬNo,тАЭ said Elizabeth-Jane firmly, in her revulsion of feeling. тАЬHe knew your dispositionтБатАФyou always were so trusting, father; IтАЩve heard my mother say so hundreds of timesтБатАФand he did it to wrong you. After weaning me from you these five years by saying he was my father, he should not have done this.тАЭ

Thus they conversed; and there was nobody to set before Elizabeth any extenuation of the absent oneтАЩs deceit. Even had he been present Henchard might scarce have pleaded it, so little did he value himself or his good name.
тАЬWell, wellтБатАФnever mindтБатАФit is all over and past,тАЭ said Newson good-naturedly. тАЬNow, about this wedding again.тАЭ
